Summary: In B cell progenitors, Pax5 specifies B cell identity and mediates lineage commitment. Mechanistically, the NAD+-dependent deacetylase SIRT7 directly deacetylates Pax5 at K198, which prevents Pax5 proteasomal degradation, increases Pax5 chromatin occupancy and enhances its transcriptional (especially repressive) activity; conversely, the acetyltransferase PCAF acetylates K198 and reduces Pax5 stability. Outcome: SIRT7-dependent dynamic deacetylation of Pax5 K198 is required for repression of lineage-inappropriate genes, the pro-B-to-pre-B transition and normal B lymphopoiesis in mice; a K198 deacetylation mimic restores lineage commitment (and rescues Sirt7−/− defects) but neither fixed deacetylated nor acetylated mimics restore full B cell differentiation in Pax5−/− cells. The SIRT7–Pax5 relationship is conserved in human B-ALL, where SIRT7 protein correlates with PAX5 levels and with better prognosis.
References: Gamez-Garcia, A., Espinosa-Alcantud, M., Bueno-Costa, A., Alari-Pahissa, E., Marazuela-Duque, A., Thackray, J. K., Ray, C., Berenguer, C., Kumari, P., Bech, J. J., Braun, T., Ianni, A., Tischfield, J. A., Serrano, L., Esteller, M., Sardina, J. L., De La Torre, C., Sigvardsson, M., Vazquez, B. N., & Vaquero, A. (2024). A SIRT7-dependent acetylation switch regulates early B cell differentiation and lineage commitment through Pax5. Nat Immunol, 25(12), 2308-2319. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41590-024-01995-7
